coredata.dc:title: Glucosamine-conjugated graphene quantum dots as versatile and pH-sensitive nanocarriers for enhanced delivery of curcumin targeting to breast cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Ghanbari, Narges
Salehi, Zeinab
Khodadadi, Abbas Ali
Shokrgozar, Mohammad Ali
Saboury, Ali Akbar
coredata.dc:description: Applying multifunctional nanocarriers, comprising specifically traceable and tumor targeting moieties, has significantly increased in cancer theranostics. Herein, a novel targeted, trackable, and pH-responsive drug delivery system was fabricated based on glucosamine (GlcN) conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded by hydrophobic anticancer agent, curcumin (Cur), to evaluate its targeting and cytotoxicity potential against breast cancer cells with overexpression of GlcN receptors. The biocompatible photoluminescent GQDs were synthesized from graphene oxide through the green and facile oxidizing method. The structural and spectral characterizations of the as-prepared GQDs and Cur/GlcN-GQDs were investigated. The GQDs sizes were within 20–30 nm and showed less than ten layers. A pH-sensitive and sustained release behavior was also observed for the Cur loaded nanocarrier with a total release of 37% at pH 5.5 and 17% at pH 7.4 after 150 h. In vitro cellular uptake studies through fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry exhibited stronger fluorescence for the targeted nanocarrier against MCF-7 cells compared to the non-targeted one, owing to higher cellular internalization via GlcN receptor-mediated endocytosis. Furthermore, the MTT assay results demonstrated the nontoxicity of the bare nanocarrier with the cell viability of above 94% even at concentrations as high as 50 μg·ml−1, while the Cur/GlcN-GQDs exhibited much more cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells compared to Cur/GQDs. It is reasonable to conclude that this advanced multifunctional nano-assembly offers superior potential for breast cancer cell-targeted delivery.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GQDs, (b) FESEM image of GQDs, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs, the absorption band at 232 nm corresponds to π → π* of CC bonds, and PL spectra of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs in deionized water at different excitation wavelengths from 410 nm to 490 nm, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength are 460 and 526 nm, respectively. PL spectra indicate the excitation-dependent photoluminescence of GQDs. (d) Photoluminescence spectra of GlcN-GQDs and GQDs at the same concentration of GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. An increase in intensity and a redshift in PL spectrum of GQDs are observed after its conjugation with GlcN (e) FTIR spectra of GQDs, GlcN, GlcN-GQDs, Cur and Cur/GlcN-GQDs. a.u. means Arbitrary Unit.

Cumulative release of Cur from Cur/GlcN-GQDs in PBS (pH values of 5.5 and 7.4) at 37 °C. A pH-responsive and sustained drug release behavior is observed for the nano-assembly. About 40% and 17% of Cur is dissociated from Cur/GlcN-GQDs after 150 h at pH values of 5.5 and 7.4, respectively. Data are displayed as the means ± SD with n = 3.

In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs following the MTT assay: (a) MCF-7 cells, (b) fibroblast normal cells. Anticancer activity of Cur/GlcN-GQDs, Cur/GQDs, and free Cur following the MTT assay: (c) MCF-7 cells, (d) fibroblast normal cells. The cells exposed to different concentrations (6.25, 12.5, 25, and 50 μg·ml−1) of GQDs for 48 h show no obvious toxicity. Cells were also exposed to different concentrations (6.25, 12.5, 25, and 50 μg·ml−1) of free Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/GlcN-GQDs for 48 h. A higher cytotoxicity is detected for the targeted nano-assembly against MCF-7 cells rather than non-targeted one at the same Cur dosage. No significant difference in cytotoxicity is detected against fibroblast normal cells between the targeted and non-targeted nano-assemblies. Data were displayed as means ± SD with n = 3.

Apoptosis level measurement by flow cytometry. Apoptotic cells were detected by flow cytometry with Annexin V-FITC/PI. (a) Control cells, (b) cells treated with GQDs sample, GQDs concentration of 50 μg·ml−1.

(a) Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after incubation with GQDs for 4 h and excitation at 488 nm, (b) fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after incubation with GQDs for 24 h and excitation at 488 nm, (c) fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after incubation with GlcN-GQDs for 24 h and excitation at 488 nm (GQDs concentration of 50 μg·ml−1). Scale bar: 20 μm.

head.ce:title: Glucosamine-conjugated graphene quantum dots as versatile and pH-sensitive nanocarriers for enhanced delivery of curcumin targeting to breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Applying multifunctional nanocarriers, comprising specifically traceable and tumor targeting moieties, has significantly increased in cancer theranostics. Herein, a novel targeted, trackable, and pH-responsive drug delivery system was fabricated based on glucosamine (GlcN) conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded by hydrophobic anticancer agent, curcumin (Cur), to evaluate its targeting and cytotoxicity potential against breast cancer cells with overexpression of GlcN receptors. The biocompatible photoluminescent GQDs were synthesized from graphene oxide through the green and facile oxidizing method. The structural and spectral characterizations of the as-prepared GQDs and Cur/GlcN-GQDs were investigated. The GQDs sizes were within 20–30 nm and showed less than ten layers. A pH-sensitive and sustained release behavior was also observed for the Cur loaded nanocarrier with a total release of 37% at pH 5.5 and 17% at pH 7.4 after 150 h. In vitro cellular uptake studies through fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry exhibited stronger fluorescence for the targeted nanocarrier against MCF-7 cells compared to the non-targeted one, owing to higher cellular internalization via GlcN receptor-mediated endocytosis. Furthermore, the MTT assay results demonstrated the nontoxicity of the bare nanocarrier with the cell viability of above 94% even at concentrations as high as 50 μg·ml−1, while the Cur/GlcN-GQDs exhibited much more cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells compared to Cur/GQDs. It is reasonable to conclude that this advanced multifunctional nano-assembly offers superior potential for breast cancer cell-targeted delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Fabrication of targeted nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems with their exclusive or preferable accumulation in tumor cells offers superior potential in breast cancer diagnosis and therapies [1–4]. The significance of targeted drug delivery becomes more pronounced, considering the increasing amount of cancer-related mortality as well as the shortcomings associated with prevalent cancer-therapy strategies. Surgical removal of all involved tissues, particularly in metastatic cancer cases, is impractical; Also, conventionally-used chemotherapy drugs suffer from poor pharmacokinetics and physicochemical properties as well as nonspecific targeting and premature release behavior which lead to low therapeutic efficacy and serious side effects. To address these limitations, tumor-targeting nanocarriers with favorable properties of traceability and pH-sensitivity release behavior have been developed and applied in recent years cancer treatments [5–8]. Among numerous nanocarriers, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of the most effective ones, due to their exceptional and outstanding characteristics.

GQDs are the zero-dimensional generation of carbon nanostructures, gathering desirable properties of graphene and semiconductor quantum dots simultaneously [9,10]. Similar to other graphene derivatives, GQDs have high drug loading capacity, especially for aromatic drugs, through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, as reported by numerous studies [11–13]. Though both GQDs and graphene oxide (GO) possess plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups, which is beneficial for their further functionalization and makes them highly soluble and stable in an aqueous system [14], several pieces of research suggest that GQDs have superior properties in comparison with GO. Most importantly, their intrinsic photoluminescence originating from quantum confinement and edge effects is highly applicable in cell imaging, traceability of drug routes, and cellular uptake [15–19]. Moreover, they have higher stability in the physiological environment, less toxicity, more bioavailability, and cellular uptake owing to their smaller size compared to GO [20–23]. On the other hand, GQDs have several points in common with various carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots, which have recently gained attention for application in drug delivery systems [24–26]. They all benefit from facile preparation and photochemical stability, but GQDs have lower toxicity compared to semiconductor quantum dots, which makes them preferable for biomedical applications.

Up till now, extensive synthetic methods have been applied for the preparation of GQDs including hydrothermal, solvothermal, acidic exfoliation, chemical oxidation, and electrochemical oxidation [27–32]. Much efforts have been recently devoted to the synthesis of GQDs from a facile eco-friendly and cost-inexpensive method, in which H2O2 is being used as an oxidative agent instead of conventional strong acids and oxidants. For instance, Jiang et al. synthesized biocompatible amine-functionalized GQDs from GO, NH3, and H2O2 under a mild condition followed by column chromatography for their separation [33]. In another research, Zhu et al. prepared GQDs from GO applying a clean oxidizing agent of H2O2 and catalyst of W18O49. No by-products were left in this method and no further GQDs purification was needed before their application in fluorescent imaging [34]. More recently, Xin et al. reported a novel method of preparation of nitrogen-doped GQDs from graphite as a precursor, ammonium persulfate as a nitrogen source, and H2O2 as an oxidant to be used in bioimaging application [35]. Although GQDs can be easily constructed, are highly biocompatible, show high drug loading capacity for anticancer drugs, and are traceable due to their photoluminescence, it is to be noted that without targeting capability, the challenge of severe side effects of chemotherapeutic drugs on normal cells remains unresolved. Therefore, it is highly essential to fabricate targeted GQDs.

For targeted delivery, the nanocarrier is conjugated with specific antibodies/ligands which are capable of recognizing tumor-specific/associated antigens on cancer cells through ligand-receptor, antibody-antigen, or lectin-carbohydrate interactions [36]. Considerable progress has already been made in integrated therapy based on targeted drug delivery, yet very few investigations are available on applying targeting agents with GQDs for cancer therapy. Some of the targeting agents that have been used with GQDs nanocarriers are Anti-HER2 [37], hyaluronic acid (HA) [38], biotin (BTN) [39], folic acid (FA) [12,40], RGD [41], and aptamer [42]. Glucosamine (GlcN) is an amino monosaccharide with proper biocompatibility, solubility, and tumor targeting ability [43,44] via binding to GLUTs receptor which is a kind of transmembrane tumor-associated protein for glucose transport and is overexpressed in various cancers such as breast cancer [45–47]. Furthermore, GlcN is rich in functional groups, proper for conjugation with nanocarriers while maintaining its original bioactivity. The mentioned potential of GQDs and GlcN prompted our group to develop a novel biocompatible, trackable, and pH-responsive nanocarrier based on GlcN-conjugated GQDs with the ability to target to breast cancer.

To further illustrate the efficiency of targeted nanocarrier of GlcN-GQDs, curcumin (Cur) was chosen as a model drug for loading. The anticancer activity of Cur is effective in controlling many types of cancers, including breast [48–50], lungs [51], colon [52], gastric [53], and liver [54] cancers as reported in several investigations. However, the curcumin clinical application is hindered due to its poor water solubility, low bioavailability, and rapid metabolism. To overcome these inherent limitations, Cur has been loaded on a variety of nanocarriers through different methods of drug loading such as encapsulation, physical absorption, and covalent interaction [54–56]. There are also few reports on using GQDs as a Cur nanocarrier in cancer treatments [57,58]. Recently, De et al. developed a stable complex of graphene derivatives with Cur in which Cur was loaded onto GO and GQDs through π-π stacking and showed high loading efficiency. However, the complexes' preferentially targeting ability towards cancer cells compared to normal cells was not investigated [58]. Hence, it seems that more studies are needed on applying targeted GQDs for Cur smart and efficient delivery. Thus, in this work, Cur was loaded onto GlcN-conjugated GQDs for the first time, to investigate the targeting ability of the GlcN-GQDs towards breast cancer cells as well as mitigating the drawbacks associated with free Cur application.

The focus of the present study lies on GlcN-targeted GQDs, as a nanocarrier for Cur delivery to MCF-7 cancer cells, to investigate the effectiveness of GlcN as a targeting agent, which has not been previously studied. GlcN is anchored to GQDs via amide covalent bonds followed by non-covalent loading of Cur as an anticancer agent to prepare Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly. Next, the Cur release from this nano-assembly was investigated at two pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 to study the pH-sensitivity of the drug delivery system. Fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry were then applied for quantitatively and qualitatively evaluating the in vitro cellular uptake of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs by MCF-7 cells. Eventually, the cytotoxicity of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs as bare nanocarriers and Cur/GQDs and Cur/GlcN-GQDs as drug-loaded nanocarriers on human breast cancer cells, MCF-7, and normal fibroblast cells were investigated through MTT assay.

Graphite powder and GlcN were purchased from Fluka and Solarbio, respectively. Cur, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide, ammonia, and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Merck. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, DMEM medium, and RPMI-1640 medium were purchased from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA). The dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa) was obtained from Spectrum Labs Co (USA) and the Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter units (10 kDa and 3 kDa) were purchased from Merck Millipore. The human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was obtained from the cell bank of Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran. The fibroblast cells were obtained from the cell culture lab of the Biological Sciences Center of Tehran University. All solutions were prepared in deionized water or phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) unless otherwise specified.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips, CM300, Netherland), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss, SIGMA VP-50, Germany), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, JPK instruments, NanoWizard II, Germany) were applied to investigate the morphological and structural characteristics of the samples. Drops of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs were deposited on the carbon-coated copper grid, silicon wafer, and mica substrate for TEM, FESEM, and AFM analyses, respectively. Hydrodynamic size distribution and ζ-potentials were determined by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) method (ZetaSizer, Malvern Instruments, Nano ZS90, USA) on diluted aqueous solutions of samples. These samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and sonicated prior to the analysis. Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis, Varian, Carry 100 Bio, Australia) absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra (PL, Varian, Carry eclipse, Australia) of the samples were obtained to investigate the optical characterization of the samples. Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, Vector 22, Germany) was used over the range of 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 in transmission mode, using a KBr pellet for sample preparation. Elemental and chemical analysis of the nanocarrier was performed by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, Zeiss, Oxford Instrument, Germany). Cellular uptake investigations were performed by fluorescence microscopy (Olympus, BX50, Japan) and flow cytometry (FCM, BD biosciences, BD FACS Calibur, USA) under 488 nm excitation.
GO was synthesized using a previously reported method based on the improved Hummers' method [59]. The details of the synthesis method can be found in Supplementary 1.

GQDs were prepared through an eco-friendly method, in which the precursor of GO was cut into GQDs under the synergistic effects of H2O2 and NH3 [33,60]. The synthesis process is described in detail in Supplementary 2.

Covalent functionalization of GQDs was carried out via EDC/NHS activators [61]. In a typical reaction, EDC (48 mg) and NHS (16 mg) were introduced simultaneously to GQDs solution (6 ml, 0.5 mg·ml−1) and stirred for 2 h to reach a homogenous solution and activate the carboxyl group of GQDs. Then, an amine-containing agent, GlcN (2 mg) was added to the solution, and stirring was continued for 18 h at room temperature. After the reaction was completed, Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (3 kDa) was used to separate the conjugated GlcN-GQDs from free GlcN and small molecules and analyzed for the amine functional groups.

Cur is a hydrophobic substance with poor water solubility. Therefore, for the loading of Cur on GlcN-GQDs, Cur should first be dissolved in a suitable solvent and then mixed with GQDs solution in water. Among acetone, DMSO, and ethanol as alternatives of the Cur solvent, the first two have the limitation of using Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter devices despite being superior solvents for Cur. As a result, ethanol was selected, which is biocompatible and miscible with water. For Cur loading, the solution of GlcN-GQDs in DI water (1.5 ml, 1 mg·ml−1) was added to the solution of Cur in ethanol (1.5 ml, 1.25 mg·ml−1) at pH 7.4 to achieve the final Cur concentration of 0.625 mg·ml−1 and a 50:50 (v/v) mixture of ethanol to water. After stirring for 24 h in the dark, the unbounded Cur molecules were separated from the solution by centrifugation using the Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (3 kDa). The solution retained on the filter was repeatedly washed with DI water to ensure that no free Cur has remained in the filter.

To investigate the behavior of Cur release from GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly, a membrane dialysis method was carried out. In brief, a 5 ml of Cur/GlcN-GQDs sample was poured into the dialysis tubing (3.5 kDa) and sealing was checked; then it was put into a dark and sealed vessel containing phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (50 ml, 150 mM) under the physiological condition (pH 7.4 and 37 °C) and a constant mild shaking (140 rpm). Since Cur has poor solubility in the buffer solution, 0.5% w/v tween-80 was added to the release media to facilitate the Cur release process. The starting point of release was measured as soon as dialysis tubing was placed into the release media. At the desired time intervals, 1.5 ml of release media was removed for UV analysis, which was immediately refilled with an equal amount of fresh media to prevent disturbance to the sink condition. The cumulative drug released was calculated using a calibration curve according to the following equation [7]:
(1)

%

C t

i = 1

where Mtotal represents the initial total amount of Cur in the dialysis tubing; V0 and V are the initial volumes of the release media and the volume of the samples collected from release media at predetermined time intervals (V0 of 50 ml and V of 1.5 ml in the present work), respectively; Ct indicates the calculated Cur concentration in the release media at specified time t. The drug release experiment was repeated for a pH value of 5.5 as well.

MCF-7, human breast cancer cells, and normal fibroblast cells were chosen for cell viability studies. The MCF-7 cells and fibroblast cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 and DMEM, respectively, supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin and streptomycin.

The cell viabilities of the MCF-7 tumor cells and fibroblast normal cells after being treated with different formulations of Cur (free Cur, Cur/GQDs, Cur/GlcN-GQDs) and drug-free nanocarriers (GQDs, GlcN-GQDs) were evaluated by conducting the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. MTT assay is a colorimetric method for quantification of cell viability, which is based on the assessment of cell metabolic activity. Briefly, the cell suspension (50 μl) was seeded in each well of a 96-well plate at a density of 4 × 103 cells/well for MCF-7 cells and 3 × 103 cells/well for fibroblast cells and incubated overnight for cell attachment [25]. Then, 100 μl of GQDs, GlcN-GQDs (50, 25, 12.5, 6.25 μg·ml−1), and free Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/GlcN-GQDs (final Cur concentrations: 50, 25, 12.5 and 6.25 μg·ml−1) were used for treating the cells. For both cell types, the untreated cells in the culture medium were regarded as control. To prepare serial concentrations of Cur solutions, Cur was initially solubilized in ethanol; it was then diluted with RPMI 1640 and DMEM medium for MCF-7 and fibroblast cells (ethanol < 1%), respectively. Then, the MTT solution (100 μl, 0.5 mg·ml−1) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h in the dark. Finally, the supernatants were carefully removed, the formazan crystals formed by the live cells were dissolved in isopropanol (100 μl) and allowed to be incubated for another 15 min. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation, after measuring the absorbance using a microplate reader [62]:
(2)
Cell viability
%
=
OD Treated
OD Control
× 100

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of GQDs nanocarriers, Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis assay was also applied as a confirmation of MTT assay results through the flow cytometer, which can distinguish live cells from the necrotic and/or apoptotic cells. While the early apoptotic cells with exposed phosphatidylserine are stained with Annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), the late apoptotic/necrotic cells with damaged membrane are labeled with propidium iodide (PI). After seeding and incubating the MCF-7 cells (4 × 105 cells/well) on a six-well plate at 37 °C for 24 h, they were treated with GQDs (100 μg·ml−1) and incubated for another 48 h. The untreated cells in the culture medium were regarded as control. After trypsinization and centrifugation (1300 rpm, 5 min) of the cells, they were washed with fresh cold PBS to remove the cell medium. The cell pellet was then resuspended in 1× Annexin-binding buffer (500 μl). To correct the overlapped range of FITC and PI stains, the cell suspension was divided into four falcons (105 cells/falcon). While the first falcon was left unstained, Annexin V-FITC, PI, and both FITC and PI were added to the other falcons. Annexin V-FITC (5 μl) was then added to the second and fourth falcons and they were incubated for 15 min in the dark at room temperature. After the addition of 1× Annexin-binding buffer (1000 μl) to these falcons and centrifugation (1300 rpm, 5 min), 1× Annexin-binding buffer (250 μl) was again added to the cell pellet. PI (3 μl) was added to the third and fourth falcons. The labeled samples were immediately red by flow cytometer and analyzed using FlowJo software. Early apoptotic (Annexin V-positive and PI-negative), late apoptotic (Annexin V-positive and PI-positive), and necrotic (Annexin V-negative and PI-positive) cells were included in total cell death determinations.
Cellular uptakes of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs by MCF-7 cells were assessed both qualitatively and quantitatively by fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry, respectively.

First, to investigate the time-dependent cellular uptake behavior of GQDs, it was incubated with MCF-7 cells for 4 and 24 h. Then, to evaluate the selective uptake of GlcN-GQDs compared to GQDs, MCF-7 cells with overexpression of GLUTs receptor were used. Initially, cells were cultured in a 24-well plate at a density of 2 × 105 cells/well. After incubation for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2-containing atmosphere, the medium was removed and cells were treated with GQDs and GlcN-GQDs (50 μg·ml−1). Cells were then allowed to be incubated for 24 h. After removing the cell medium, they were washed three times with fresh cold PBS to remove the nanoparticles not internalized into the cells. The cells were then fixed for 20 min in paraformaldehyde (PFA 4% v/v in PBS) at room temperature. After washing with PBS for removing traces of the fixation agent, the cells were imaged by using a fluorescence microscope under excitation of 488 nm.

For the flow cytometry assay, MCF-7 cells (2 × 105 cells/well) were initially seeded in a 24-well plate. Allowing them overnight to adhere to the plate at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2-containing atmosphere, the cells were treated with GQDs and GlcN-GQDs for 24 h at the same concentration as used in the fluorescence microscopy study (50 μg·ml−1). The cells were then washed three times with fresh cold PBS before trypsinization. After complete cell detachment, the complete medium was added; they were centrifuged for 5 min at 1300 rpm at 25 °C, redispersed in the cell culture medium, and analyzed using a flow cytometer. This analysis was applied to estimate the cellular uptake, and FlowJo VX software was used to analyze the obtained data.

Morphology and size of the synthesized GQDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1a), FESEM (Fig. 1b), and AFM (Fig. S1a). The TEM bright-field image displays a disordered quasi-hexagonal shape of GQDs with a lateral size of about 20–30 nm which correlates well with the overall sizes observed in the FESEM image (Fig. 1b). The AFM height profile (Fig. S1b) reveals that the typical topographic height of the GQDs is in the range of 1–2.5 nm, suggesting that most of the GQDs have below 10 graphene layers considering the interlayer spacing of graphene (0.34 nm). The size distribution profile of GQDs nanocarriers was also measured by a dynamic light scattering (DLS) method, which proves a relatively narrow size distribution of the GQDs with a hydrodynamic size of 24.4 nm (Fig. S2). The size of GQDs obtained by TEM, FESEM, and DLS are well consistent with each other, representing an average size of about 20–30 nm that is the optimal size range for nanoparticles used as drug carriers [63,64].

The surface charge of GQDs is of great importance, not only in solubility and stability of the system but also in cell-nanoparticle interactions in traversing the cell membrane. The measured zeta potential of the GQDs in the aqueous dispersion is negative (−20.7 mV) (Fig. S3), arising from a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which make GQDs highly dispersible and stable in aqueous systems. The EDS (Fig. S4) analysis revealed the O/C and N/C atomic ratio of 0.91 and 0.68, respectively, both values of which are relatively high compared to similar studies [33,65]. This indicates the presence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, respectively, which is desirable for further functionalizations.

UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy were carried out to explore the optical properties of GQDs. The UV–Vis absorption, the spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1c), shows a typical absorption band around 232 nm, which corresponds to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (characteristic of a polycyclic aromatic system) [19,28] and a long tail extending into the visible range. According to PL studies (Fig. 1c), the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the GQDs is 460 and 526 nm, respectively, which is consistent with its bright green emission under excitation in UV light. Moreover, the PL spectra are generally broad and dependent on excitation wavelengths for most luminescent carbon nanoparticles. A clear redshift from 526 to 540 nm is observed with an increase in the excitation wavelength from 460 to 490 nm. The exact photoluminescence mechanism of GQDs is an unclear issue, assumed to be synergistically affected by the method of synthesis, size, functionalization, surface defects, and zigzag sites [65–67].

FTIR spectra (Fig. 1e) were used to investigate the surface functional groups of GQDs. FTIR analysis confirms the functionalization of GQDs with various oxygen-containing functional groups and amine groups, which renders GQDs remarkably hydrophilic and stable in aqueous systems for drug loading. The two peaks in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The peaks at 1723, 1440, and 1618 cm−1 are associated with the vibrational bands of CO and OH in the carboxylic group and CC, respectively [26]. The peaks that appeared at 1300 and 1109 cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group and CN groups, respectively. In general, the FTIR analyses indicate that nitrogen-containing functional groups such as amine and oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl are present on the GQDs structure that facilitate the subsequent functionalizations.

The functionalization of GQDs with GlcN as a targeting agent was done via an EDC/NHS activation reaction. As can be observed in the FTIR spectra of GlcN-GQDs (Fig. 1e), conjugation of GlcN to GQDs through amide bond formation was confirmed by the appearance of characteristic NH-CO stretching vibration at 1645 cm−1. Moreover, the two peaks of 1537 and 1250 cm−1 attributed to the vibrational bands of NH and CN of GlcN molecules can be observed at a higher wavelength of 1563 cm−1 and 1270 cm−1, respectively, in the FTIR spectra of GlcN-GQDs. Also, PL studies (Fig. 1d) indicate an increase in intensity and a redshift in the PL spectrum location of GlcN-GQDs at 514 nm compared to GQDs at 508 nm when excited at 430 nm, which again provide support for the correct conjugation of GQDs [68].

Possessing a large number of delocalized π electrons, graphene structures can bind to anticancer drugs with an aromatic ring by π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions [69]. FTIR spectra were used to confirm the loading of Cur on GlcN-GQDs. Fig. 1e demonstrates the FTIR spectra of Cur, GlcN-GQDs, and Cur/GlcN-GQDs. The assumption of Cur absorption on GQDs by hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking is supported by the increased peak intensity of the common groups such as CH at 2965 cm−1 and OH at 3383 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of Cur from Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The behavior of Cur release from Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly was investigated at 37 °C throughout 150 h at two pH values of 7.4 and 5.5 as a representative of physiological pH of whole-body fluid and intracellular lysosomes and endosomes, respectively, to determine whether the release mechanism is pH-dependent. Fig. 2 illustrates the profiles of cumulative Cur release from the Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly, which verify the pH-dependent and sustained drug release behavior. As it is observed, within the first 7 h, less than 10% of Cur was released from the nano-assembly at both pH values, which is indicative of no significant burst release for this drug delivery system. The initial rate of Cur release is relatively high up to 30 h with a negligible difference at two pH values. This can be attributed to the detachment of those Cur molecules stacked on each other with no strong interactions, which are not deeply affected by pH variations. The Cur release rate is still ascending with time at pH 5.5 with a lower slope compared to the beginning of the release process, which finally becomes nearly constant after 130 h. However, the Cur release rate becomes constant at pH 7.4 after two days. By comparing the final cumulative release amounts of 40% and 17% at pH values of 5.5 and 7.4, respectively, it can be concluded that acidic condition accelerates Cur releases, which is in good agreement with previous reports [50,51,70,71]. This can be explained by considering the drug-nanocarrier interactions and the effect of pH variations. Drug release mechanisms can be grouped into four categories of diffusion-controlled, solvent-controlled, degradation-controlled, and stimuli-controlled release. Given the planar structure of the GlcN-GQDs nanocarrier as well as non-covalent loading, i.e. physical adsorption of Cur on its surface, none of the first three mechanisms is responsible for the release of Cur from Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly. Therefore, the stimuli-controlled release mechanism should then be checked for the release process. One of the most important external stimuli of drug release is pH. The pH-sensitivity is a highly remarkable property of a drug delivery system considering the acidic pH of solid tumors in contrast with the neutral pH of the whole body, which can lead to a controlled-release behavior of the drug. The interactions between Cur and GlcN-GQDs are of π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding type, both of which are influenced by pH change. GlcN-GQDs and Cur have several hydrogen-containing groups such as -COOH, -OH, and -NH2. Different kinds of hydrogen bonding can be predicted between any two groups. These hydrogen bonding interactions have excellent stability under neutral conditions, which leads to a deficient release. In contrast, the protonation of the OH group of Cur in acidic media caused the weakening of hydrophobic and H-bonding interaction between the nanocarrier and Cur as well as increased hydrophilicity and solubility of Cur [72,73]. So, it is reasonable to assume that Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly has proper stability within blood circulation and in the vicinity of normal tissues (pH 7.4) until they reach the tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5–7.0). After the nano-assembly enters the cell, the completely acidic environment of endosomes and lysosomes (pH 5.0) accelerates the Cur release which is desirable as a drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of Cur release from Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly
ce:section.ce:para:
Various kinetic models can be used to analyze the release of a drug from a nano- assembly. Here, the cur cumulative release data were fitted to first-order (Eq. (3)), pseudo-second-order (Eq. (4)), and Higuchi (Eq. (5)) models as common models:

In all equations, Q0, Qe, and Qt are the amounts of cur released initially, at equilibrium, and at time t, respectively; k is the associated release rate constant. The linear forms of the above-mentioned models are plotted in Figs. S5 and S6 for pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively, while the regression data are listed in Table S1a. By comparing the evaluated regression coefficients, it can be inferred that the experimental data of Cur release from Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly at pH 5.5 and 7.4 has the best compliance with the pseudo-second-order model which is supported by similar studies reported in the literature [74,75].

As pointed out earlier, there is a negligible difference between the amount of Cur release at two pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 at the very beginning of the experiment. This can be attributed to the detachment of those Cur molecules that have been stacked on each other with no strong interactions, which is not affected by pH variations. To check this hypothesis, the experimental data of both pH values were divided into two ranges: the period before the 7th hour (part 1) and the period from the 7th hour to the end of the experiment (part 2). Next, the data of parts 1 and 2 were fitted to the first-order and pseudo-second-order model, respectively, and the results are summarized in Table S1b. The first-order model is not appropriate for the whole range of the experiment although the data of part 1fitted very well with the model. Moreover, the pseudo-second-order model shows better regression coefficients when the data of part 1 are excluded from the whole range of data. This verifies that the initial release of Cur from the nano-assembly is not governed by pH variations or any other special interaction. This completely matches the first-order model applications. On the other hand, pseudo-second-order model complies well with those systems in which drug release is governed by a definite factor, like pH.

All nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems should be checked for safety and non-toxicity, an essential factor for biomedical applications. The cytotoxicity effects of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs against MCF-7, the human breast cancer cell line, and normal fibroblast cell lines were evaluated by MTT assay as a simple and potent tool. As illustrated in Fig. 3a and b, after incubation for 48 h, the cell viability of both cell lines treated with GQDs and GlcN-GQDs nanocarriers remains above 94% even at high concentrations (50 μg·ml−1). However, slightly higher cell viability can be seen for GlcN-GQDs at most concentrations in comparison to bare GQDs, especially for MCF-7 cells, indicating that the attachment of GlcN to GQDs improves its biocompatibility [76]. So, no apparent cytotoxicity is observed for drug-free nanocarriers with desirable biocompatibility which is in complete agreement with similar studies [17,21,39,41]. MTT assay was also performed to evaluate the anticancer potential of Cur/GlcN-GQDs, Cur/GQDs, and free Cur at a treatment time of 48 h. For both MCF-7 cells and fibroblast cells, the cell viabilities of Cur/GlcN-GQDs and Cur/GQDs decrease with the increase in Cur concentration (Fig. 3c and d). Also, by comparing the cell viability results of Cur/GlcN-GQDs and Cur/GQDs with the same Cur dosage for each cell line, higher cytotoxicity can be seen for the targeted nano-assembly against MCF-7 cells rather than non-targeted one, while nearly identical cytotoxicity is observed for both nano-assemblies against fibroblast cells. For example, at Cur equivalent dosage of 50 μg·ml−1, the cell viability of MCF-7 cells treated with Cur/GlcN-GQDs and Cur/GQDs was about 40% and 55%, respectively. However, the cell viability of fibroblast cells treated with Cur/GlcN-GQDs and Cur/GQDs was about 54% and 58%, respectively. This can easily be explained by considering that higher levels of Gluts receptors exist in MCF-7 cells compared to fibroblast cells [44,77] and GlcN, as a targeting ligand, enhances cellular uptake of its attached nanocarrier through receptor-mediated endocytosis. The concentration required for 50% inhibition of cellular growth (IC50) for free Cur is lower than that for Cur/GlcN-GQDs for both cell lines, suggesting that the free Cur showed higher cytotoxicity under the same condition compared to Cur/GlcN-GQDs. This might be ascribed to their different uptake mechanisms. The Cur molecules enter the cells directly via diffusion while the internalization for Cur/GlcN-GQDs is via receptor-mediated endocytosis [78] which is consequently associated with sustained release of Cur from its nano-assembly. In addition, as can be seen in Fig. 2, the cumulative Cur releases hardly reach 40% after 48 h that cannot obviously compete with free Cur. Another significant point deduced by comparing the two cell line results is that there is not much difference between free Cur cytotoxicity against MCF-7 and fibroblast cells. However, when conjugated with the targeting agent of GlcN, the cytotoxicity of Cur/GlcN-GQDs significantly decreases against normal fibroblast cell compared to cancerous cell, especially at higher Cur doses, which is desirable.

Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis assay was also performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of GQDs nanocarriers [79,80]. A minor percentage of cells of 8.13% was found in the necrotic zone (Fig. 4 ). Compared to control (unstained cells), GQDs could not induce any significant apoptosis or necrosis of MCF-7 cells after 48 h even at a concentration of 50 μg·ml−1, which is perfectly consistent with MTT assay, proving the non-toxicity of GQDs as a nanocarrier.

To evaluate the GLUTs-targeting characteristics of GlcN-GQDs nanocarrier, fluorescence microscopy studies and the flow cytometry analysis were applied in MCF-7 cells as target cells, with over-expressed GLUTs. To quantify the cellular uptake behavior of the nanocarrier over time, MCF-7 cells were treated with GQDs at two different incubation times of 4 h and 24 h. Fluorescence microscopy images (Fig. 5a) show that the GQDs fluorescence becomes more intense by increasing the time from 4 h to 24 h, revealing the time-dependent behavior of GQDs cellular uptake. Fig. 5b and c illustrates the fluorescence intensity of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs uptake by MCF-7 cells after an incubation time of 24 h with excitation at 488 nm. Significant fluorescence is observed for the two nanocarriers, and fluorescence of GlcN-GQDs is brighter compared to GQDs. It should be noted that a control experiment was carried out to ensure that the detected green fluorescence originated from the internalized nanocarriers by the cells rather than the autofluorescence of the cells. Flow cytometry studies were also performed to evaluate the internalization of the two nanocarriers of GQDs and GlcN-GQDs by MCF-7 cancer cells after an incubation time of 24 h under an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. A fluorescence signal was observed for MCF-7 cells treated with GQDs and GlcN-GQDs (16.8% and 23%, respectively). These results are in complete agreement with fluorescence microscopy observations, both indicating that internalization of GlcN-GQDs via receptor-mediated endocytosis is higher than GQDs, due to the overexpression of GLUTs receptor on MCF-7 cells. As cancer cells multiply, the need and subsequent uptake of oxygen and nutrients such as glucose increase dramatically for their survival, angiogenesis, and proliferation [81]. This leads to an increase of aerobic glycolysis as well as overexpression of GLUTs receptors. GlcN ligand can bind to GLUTs through ligand-receptor interactions, which can then be internalized by tumor cells.

In the present study, the photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized; glucosamine (GlcN), as a targeting agent, was conjugated onto the GQDs (GlcN-GQDs); curcumin (Cur), as an anticancer agent, was then loaded onto GlcN-GQDs, and the resulting nano-assembly was used for killing MCF-7 breast cancer cells. A pH-responsive and sustained drug release behavior is observed for the nano-assembly. Much higher releases were observed at an acidic condition, as compared to a neutral one. This is desirable, considering the acidic environment of tumors. In vitro cellular uptake studies through fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry reveal a higher internalization of the GlcN conjugated GQDs nanocarrier by the MCF-7 cells compared to the non-targeted one. Furthermore, the MTT assay results indicate negligible cytotoxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cell line and fibroblast normal cells for drug-free nanocarrier. Higher cytotoxicity for Cur/GlcN-GQDs nano-assembly is observed against MCF-7 cells in comparison to the Cur/GQDs with a little cytotoxicity against normal cell which again confirms the potential role of GlcN as a targeting agent. The newly synthesized nano-assembly is certainly preferential for drug delivery applications compared to the free drug since it represents sustained and controlled drug release behavior with lower cytotoxicity against normal cells, which mitigate its side effects.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors wish to confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome. The authors confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. The authors confirm that this manuscript has not been submitted to, nor is under review at, another journal or other publishing venues. The authors also confirm that they have no affiliation with any organization with a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript. The authors further confirm that they have considered the protection of intellectual property associated with this work and that there are no impediments to publication, including the timing of publication, concerning intellectual property. In so doing, the authors confirm that they have followed the regulations of our institutions concerning intellectual property.
